one_line_takeaway: CUD is a pre-unlearning, circuit-based difficulty score (from EAP-IG circuits) that reliably stratifies TOFU/LLM-rec forget samples into easy/hard subsets whose unlearning-efficacy gap reaches about 14-20 points across five unlearning methods, but it is a mechanistic-interpretability contribution to machine unlearning, not an AI-security paper -- it defines no threat model or attacker/defender evaluation.
key_finding: On TOFU (forget10, 400 forget samples, Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct), replacing the default forget set with CUD's easy set improves average unlearning efficacy by +3.3 points (range +1.8 to +7.0 across GradDiff/NPO/SimNPO/UNDIAL) while largely preserving retain (+1.3) and general-knowledge (-0.2) metrics; replacing it with CUD's hard set drops average unlearning efficacy by -14.1 points (up to -18.3 for NPO) with p<=1e-13 in the hard-set comparisons, and also degrades retain (-2.4) and general-knowledge (-2.4) performance, evidencing that harder samples are more entangled with retained representations. These separations hold (with modest magnitude variation, -10.6 to -14.1 for hard sets and +2.4 to +3.7 for easy sets) across cosine, Jaccard, and Hamming similarity variants of CUD (Table 3). On LLM-recommendation unlearning (Llama3, MovieLens-based split, 500 forget samples), the easy set outperforms the default split by roughly 5.5-6.9 points unlearning efficacy and the paper separately states the easy set beats a randomly selected forget set by 7.2%, while the hard set underperforms the default split by about 19.9-20.4 points (Tables 4-5). Mechanistically, easy and hard circuits show a statistically different edge-frequency distribution (p=0.01, Figure 4): easy circuits concentrate reuse on a compact set of high-frequency early/mid-layer MLP edges, hard circuits spread mass over a flatter, more diffuse set of deeper, output-proximal and attention-involving edges (Table 6 lists the specific top-10 unique edges for each group with frequency deltas). CUD correlates only weakly with the existing Memory Removal Difficulty metric (rho=-0.27, Figure 5), which the authors interpret as CUD capturing a functionally distinct, structural notion of difficulty rather than local parameter-perturbation sensitivity.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (not related work) is Circuit-guided Unlearning Difficulty (CUD): a continuous, pre-unlearning, model-internal metric that scores how hard an individual sample will be to unlearn from an LLM, computed purely from mechanistic-interpretability circuits extracted from the original (pre-unlearning) model. The method has two steps: (1) find two 'reference' forget-set circuits, an easy-to-unlearn anchor C_E and a hard-to-unlearn anchor C_H, by using the bi-level optimization of Fan et al. 2024a (max/min loss-after-unlearning objectives, Eqs. 5-6, repeated across 5 seeds and intersected for stability) to pick stable easy/hard sample subsets, then running Edge Attribution Patching with Integrated Gradients (EAP-IG, from prior work by Hanna et al. 2024) to extract each subset's aggregated circuit; (2) for any query sample z_i, extract its own circuit C_i via the same EAP-IG procedure, represent circuits as binary edge-presence matrices, compute similarity of vec(C_i) to vec(C_E) and vec(C_H), and combine them into CUD(z_i) = (1-s_E) / [(1-s_E)+(1-s_H)] in [0,1], where values near 0 indicate easy-to-unlearn and near 1 indicate hard-to-unlearn. The paper also reports a second contribution: a mechanistic characterization showing that easy-to-unlearn samples rely on shallow, compact, highly-reused early/mid-layer MLP edges (e.g., input->m0, input->m1, m0->m2, m2->m5/m6/m8), while hard-to-unlearn samples rely on deeper, more diffuse, output-proximal edges including direct logit connections and attention-mediated routing (e.g., m6->m12, m9->logits, m10->logits, m11->m13/m15, m6->a7.h2<v>).
threat_model: None. This is not an attacker/defender security paper. There is no adversary, attack surface, or exploit; the paper's mention of unlearning's motivations (removing copyrighted or PII data, compliance) is background motivation, not a studied threat model. No security claims (attack success rate, membership-inference robustness, jailbreak resistance, etc.) are evaluated for CUD itself.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitation: CUD requires circuit discovery, which is computationally expensive, so it is not intended as an online/per-iteration diagnostic over an entire retain set; it is positioned as an offline, pre-unlearning analysis tool, with cost amortization (circuit reuse/caching) and cheaper approximations left to future work. Additional limitations evident from the text itself: experiments are confined to a single small backbone (Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct) and two benchmark domains (TOFU fictitious-author unlearning and one LLM-recommendation unlearning setup); the reference easy/hard anchor circuits are themselves derived using one specific bi-level worst-case selection procedure (Fan et al. 2024a) and one MU loss family per ablation, so CUD's stability is checked across only two loss choices (GradDiff, UNDIAL) rather than a broader set; and the CUD score depends on binarized edge-presence matrices from EAP-IG circuit discovery, which itself relies on approximated integrated gradients rather than exact causal effects.

datasets_used: TOFU (Maini et al. 2024) - forget10 split, 400 forget samples, fictitious author-profile unlearning benchmark
MovieLens-1M-derived LLM-recommendation unlearning split from Wang et al. 2025 - forget-retain split, 500 forget samples
quantitative_results: TOFU average across 4 unlearning methods (GradDiff, NPO, SimNPO, UNDIAL): Hard Set by CUD unlearning efficacy 43.7 (-14.1 vs default 57.8, p<=1e-13); Easy Set by CUD unlearning efficacy 61.1 (+3.3 vs default, p~1e-3)
TOFU average: Hard Set retain 64.3 (-2.4, p<=1e-13) and general knowledge 73.1 (-2.4, p<=1e-13); Easy Set retain 68.0 (+1.3, p<=1e-13) and general knowledge 75.3 (-0.2)

Per-method unlearning efficacy on TOFU: GradDiff default 43.4 -> hard 33.2 (-10.2***), easy 50.4 (+7.0***); NPO default 54.0 -> hard 35.7 (-18.3***), easy 57.9 (+3.9***); SimNPO default 65.5 -> hard 47.9 (-17.6***), easy 67.3 (+1.8**); UNDIAL default 68.3 -> hard 57.9 (-10.4***), easy 68.6 (+0.3*)
Robustness to similarity metric on TOFU (Table 3): hard-set efficacy drop ranges -10.6 (Hamming) to -14.1 (Cosine), all p<=1e-13; easy-set efficacy gain ranges +2.4 (Hamming) to +3.7 (Jaccard)
Robustness to MU loss used to build anchors: correlation rho=0.76 between CUD scores computed from GradDiff-based vs UNDIAL-based anchor circuits (Figure 3)

LLM-recommendation unlearning (Table 4, one setting): default unlearning efficacy 76.6 -> hard set 56.2 (-20.4***), easy set 83.5 (+6.9***); retain 69.2 -> hard 53.3 (-15.9***), easy 80.9 (+11.7***); general knowledge (lower better here) 1.91 -> hard 2.21 (worse), easy 1.67 (better)
LLM-recommendation unlearning (Table 5, second setting): default 78.2 -> hard 58.3 (-19.9***), easy 83.7 (+5.5***); retain 69.2 -> hard 53.2 (-15.9***), easy 81.8 (+12.6***)
Text separately states easy set outperforms a randomly selected forget set by 7.2% in the LLM-recommendation setting
Edge-distribution difference between easy and hard circuits: statistically significant, p=0.01 (Figure 4)
CUD vs Memory Removal Difficulty (Feng et al. 2025) correlation: rho=-0.27 (weak, Figure 5)
Prior-Unlearn (pre-unlearning) baseline on TOFU: unlearning efficacy 22.0, retain 79.3, general knowledge 81.2
baselines_compared: 5 unlearning algorithms used as evaluation harness for CUD-selected sets on TOFU: GradAscent, GradDiff (Maini et al. 2024), NPO (Zhang et al. 2024), SimNPO (Fan et al. 2024b), UNDIAL (Dong et al. 2025) [Table 1 reports GradDiff/NPO/SimNPO/UNDIAL numerically; GradAscent and RMU listed among methods considered but RMU checkpoint only appears in Appendix Table 2]
E2UREC (Wang et al. 2025) and RecEraser (Chen et al. 2022) used as additional unlearning methods in the LLM-recommendation setting

Memory Removal Difficulty (MRD, Feng et al. 2025) - an existing neuroscience-inspired, online/post-hoc sample-difficulty metric, compared against CUD as an alternative difficulty notion (not a CUD ablation, but a distinct prior metric)

Worst-case forget-set selection (Fan et al. 2024a) - the paper explicitly positions CUD as a continuous generalization of this binary prior method, reusing its bi-level optimization to build CUD's own reference anchors rather than treating it as an external accuracy baseline
